q: What is the primary responsibility of kube-scheduler?
opts: Execute container images on worker nodes
Store cluster configuration and state
Assign newly created pods to nodes based on resource requirements and constraints
Maintain network rules on each node
expl: kube-scheduler watches for pods with no assigned node and selects one based on resource requests, affinity/anti-affinity, taints/tolerations, and other constraints. Option A describes the container runtime; B is etcd; D is kube-proxy.

q: etcd in a Kubernetes cluster is:
opts: A load balancer for API requests
A distributed, consistent key-value store holding all cluster state
A container runtime
The default ingress controller
expl: etcd is the source of truth for the cluster: every object, every desired state, sits here. Losing etcd without a backup means losing the cluster state, which is why regular snapshots are critical.

q: Which component runs on every worker node and ensures containers described in PodSpecs are running?
opts: kube-proxy
kubelet
kube-controller-manager
cloud-controller-manager
expl: The kubelet is the node agent. It watches the API server for pods assigned to its node and instructs the container runtime (via CRI) to run them. kube-proxy handles network rules but does not manage containers.

q: A DaemonSet is best used when you need to:
opts: Run a batch job to completion
Run N replicas of a stateless web app
Run one pod per node (or subset of nodes) for cluster-wide agents
Run a one-shot task on a schedule
expl: DaemonSets guarantee a pod on every (or selected) node — ideal for log shippers (Fluent Bit), metrics agents (node-exporter), and CNI plugins. Deployments handle stateless replicas; Jobs run to completion; CronJobs run on schedule.

q: Which Service type exposes the service on each node's IP at a static port?
opts: ClusterIP
NodePort
LoadBalancer
ExternalName
expl: NodePort opens a static port (30000-32767 by default) on every node that forwards to the Service. ClusterIP is internal-only, LoadBalancer provisions a cloud LB on top, ExternalName maps a DNS alias.

q: You need to provide a database password to a pod. Which object is most appropriate?
opts: ConfigMap
Secret
A plain env var in the pod spec
PersistentVolume
expl: Secrets are designed for sensitive data — stored base64-encoded in etcd and can be encrypted at rest. ConfigMaps are for non-sensitive config. Plaintext env vars in the pod spec end up in version control and logs.

q: A pod is stuck in Pending. Which of these is the LEAST likely cause?
opts: Insufficient node CPU or memory
Taints on nodes the pod does not tolerate
The container inside has crashed repeatedly
A PersistentVolumeClaim cannot be bound
expl: A crashing container produces CrashLoopBackOff or Error — the pod has already been scheduled and started. Pending means it has not yet been scheduled, typically due to resources, taints, or unbound PVCs.

q: A Deployment directly manages which object?
opts: Pods
ReplicaSets
StatefulSets
DaemonSets
expl: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods. During rolling updates, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet and scales down the old. You rarely manage ReplicaSets directly, but the hierarchy matters for the exam.

q: What is the defining purpose of a StatefulSet vs a Deployment?
opts: It enables horizontal scaling without an upper replica limit
It provides stable network identities (predictable pod names) and stable persistent storage per replica
It performs rolling updates faster than a Deployment by skipping readiness checks
It automatically provisions PersistentVolumes without needing a StorageClass
expl: StatefulSets give pods ordinal, predictable names (web-0, web-1, ...) and bind each replica to its own PVC that survives restarts and rescheduling. Essential for databases, brokers, and anything needing identity. A is false — both controllers scale horizontally. C is false — StatefulSets actually update more conservatively (ordered, one at a time) than Deployments. D is false — a StorageClass (or pre-provisioned PV) is still required; the StatefulSet just templates the PVCs.

q: Which kubectl command applies configuration declaratively?
opts: kubectl create -f file.yaml
kubectl run file.yaml
kubectl apply -f file.yaml
kubectl deploy file.yaml
expl: apply is declarative — creates if absent, updates if present, tracks changes via annotations. create is imperative and errors when the resource already exists.

q: Labels and selectors are used to:
opts: Enforce pod-to-pod security
Identify and group objects, enabling loose coupling between them
Encrypt data at rest
Define resource limits
expl: Labels are key/value metadata; selectors query them. This is how a Service finds its pods, how a Deployment tracks its ReplicaSet, how NetworkPolicies target workloads. It is the glue of the Kubernetes object model.

q: A namespace in Kubernetes provides:
opts: Strong security isolation by default
A scope for resource names, enabling multi-tenant division of a cluster
Encrypted pod traffic
Separate etcd instances
expl: Namespaces scope resource names and quotas but are NOT a security boundary on their own. You need NetworkPolicies, RBAC, and possibly PodSecurity admission to actually isolate tenants.

q: Which statement about Pods is true?
opts: A pod must contain exactly one container
Containers in a pod share the network namespace and can share volumes
Each container in a pod has its own IP
Pods automatically survive node failures
expl: Pods can hold one OR more containers. They share an IP, localhost, and can mount the same volumes. Pods do not survive node failure — controllers (Deployments, StatefulSets) recreate them elsewhere.

q: A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) represents:
opts: A user's request for storage that binds to a PersistentVolume
A physical disk on a node
A backup of pod data
A compressed log archive
expl: PVCs decouple pods from the specifics of storage. A pod claims storage; the PVC binds to a matching PV (static or dynamically provisioned via a StorageClass). The pod never needs to know the storage backend.

q: An Ingress resource provides:
opts: Pod-to-pod encryption
HTTP/HTTPS routing rules from outside the cluster to internal Services
DNS resolution
Container image storage
expl: Ingress defines rules (hosts, paths) routing external HTTP traffic to Services. It requires an Ingress controller (nginx, Traefik, HAProxy, etc.) to actually implement them — Ingress resources alone do nothing.

q: Which component maintains iptables/IPVS rules on nodes to implement the Service abstraction?
opts: kubelet
kube-proxy
kube-scheduler
CoreDNS
expl: kube-proxy watches Services and Endpoints on the API server and programs node network rules so traffic to a Service IP reaches a healthy pod. Modes: iptables, IPVS, nftables.

q: Taints and tolerations are used to:
opts: Attract pods to specific nodes based on node labels
Repel pods from nodes unless the pod explicitly tolerates the taint
Limit the CPU and memory a pod is allowed to consume
Automatically drain and cordon nodes during cluster upgrades
expl: A taint on a node says 'keep pods away unless they tolerate me.' Use cases: dedicated GPU nodes, reserved control-plane nodes, nodes under maintenance. A describes node affinity, which is the opposite (attraction). C describes resource requests/limits. D describes kubectl drain / cordon, which evict pods rather than gating scheduling.

q: A ReplicaSet's core responsibility is to:
opts: Ensure a specified number of pod replicas are running at any time
Perform rolling updates and rollbacks
Mount persistent storage
Schedule pods across zones
expl: ReplicaSets ensure N replicas exist — nothing more. Rolling update logic lives in the Deployment above them. If you see "ReplicaSet" in a question, think "headcount enforcer."

q: ConfigMaps should NOT be used for:
opts: Feature flags
Non-sensitive env variables
Application config files
Database passwords and API tokens
expl: ConfigMaps store data in plaintext. For secrets, use Secrets, which (a) are handled with more care in logs and CLI output and (b) support at-rest encryption when configured.

q: When a pod is deleted, its IP address:
opts: Is reserved for that pod if recreated
Is released; a new pod gets a different IP
Persists for 5 minutes
Is tied to the pod name
expl: Pod IPs are ephemeral. This is exactly why Services exist — they provide a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) even as underlying pods come and go with different IPs.

q: Which is NOT a Kubernetes workload resource?
opts: Deployment
StatefulSet
LoadBalancer
Job
expl: LoadBalancer is a Service type, not a workload. Workload resources manage pods: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet.

q: A CronJob creates:
opts: A ReplicaSet
A StatefulSet
Jobs on a time-based schedule
A Deployment
expl: CronJob → schedule (cron syntax) → Job → Pod. Each scheduled firing spawns a Job that runs to completion.

q: How do control plane components typically talk to worker nodes?
opts: Directly via SSH
Through the kubelet on each node, which watches the API server
Via etcd replication
Through kube-proxy only
expl: All communication funnels through the API server. Kubelets on nodes watch the API server for assignments and report status back. Components do not talk to each other directly.

q: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales based on:
opts: The number of nodes
CPU, memory, or custom/external metrics of pods
Time of day
Image size
expl: HPA adjusts replica count based on observed pod metrics. CPU and memory are built-in; custom metrics come via adapters (metrics-server, Prometheus Adapter, KEDA for event-driven).

q: RBAC permissions are granted by which pair of resources?
opts: Policies and Bindings
Roles/ClusterRoles and RoleBindings/ClusterRoleBindings
Users and Groups
Namespaces and Labels
expl: Roles define what can be done (namespaced) or ClusterRoles (cluster-scoped). Bindings attach those roles to subjects (users, groups, ServiceAccounts). You need both halves.

q: Which statement is true about the container image tag `latest`?
opts: Guarantees the newest secure version
Is a mutable pointer that can change, making deployments non-reproducible
Is immutable and version-pinned
Cannot be used in production
expl: `latest` is just a default tag — publishers may or may not update it. Production should pin to specific tags or, better, image digests (sha256:...) for true immutability.

q: A pod requests 250m CPU and 256Mi memory with no limits set. What does this mean?
opts: The pod cannot exceed 250m CPU
The pod is guaranteed those resources; can burst higher if node capacity allows
It will be OOM-killed if it exceeds 256Mi
It will not be scheduled
expl: Requests = scheduler reservation (guaranteed floor). Limits = cap. Without limits, a pod can use whatever the node has free — which risks noisy-neighbor effects and unpredictable OOM behavior. Best practice: set both.

q: Which probe determines if a container is ready to receive traffic from a Service?
opts: Liveness probe
Readiness probe
Startup probe
Health probe
expl: Readiness failure removes the pod from Service endpoints (but does not restart). Liveness failure restarts the container. Startup probe disables the other two until the app finishes starting.

q: Which interface standardizes how kubelet talks to container runtimes?
opts: CSI
CNI
CRI
OCI
expl: CRI = Container Runtime Interface, gRPC API between kubelet and runtimes (containerd, CRI-O). CSI is storage, CNI is networking, OCI specifies image and runtime formats.

q: CNI plugins are responsible for:
opts: Assigning IPs and configuring pod network connectivity
Scheduling pods
Storing cluster state
Providing DNS
expl: CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave) set up pod network interfaces, assign IPs, and program routes. Every cluster picks one primary CNI.

q: CSI exists so that:
opts: Containers can share storage without volumes
Storage vendors can plug into Kubernetes (and other orchestrators) without modifying core code
Pods can be auto-scaled
Containers are isolated from each other
expl: CSI decouples storage drivers from the Kubernetes release cycle. EBS, Ceph, Portworx, Longhorn all ship their own CSI drivers that K8s loads at runtime — no core patches needed.

q: OCI (Open Container Initiative) defines:
opts: Network policies
Container image format (image-spec) and runtime behavior (runtime-spec)
Kubernetes API objects
Service mesh configuration
expl: OCI is a Linux Foundation project. Its two main specs — image-spec and runtime-spec — are why an image built with Docker also runs under containerd, CRI-O, or Podman. Plus distribution-spec for registries.

q: A NetworkPolicy in Kubernetes:
opts: Is enforced automatically by the API server
Is enforced by the CNI plugin — and is silently ignored if the CNI does not support it
Only applies to ingress traffic
Replaces RBAC
expl: This is a gotcha. NetworkPolicy is a K8s resource, but enforcement is delegated to the CNI. Flannel does not enforce by default; Calico, Cilium, and Weave do. Apply a policy without an enforcing CNI and traffic flows freely.

q: What is containerd?
opts: A container orchestrator competing with Kubernetes
An industry-standard container runtime
A CNI plugin
A CI/CD tool
expl: containerd manages the container lifecycle on a host (image pull, execution, supervision). It is a CRI implementation for Kubernetes and is also what Docker uses under the hood.

q: Which Pod Security Standard is most restrictive?
opts: Privileged
Baseline
Restricted
Enforced
expl: Three levels: Privileged (unrestricted), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalation), Restricted (follows hardening best practices — non-root, no host mounts, etc.). "Enforced" is a mode, not a level.

q: Which tool scales the number of nodes in a cluster based on pending pods?
opts: HPA
VPA
Cluster Autoscaler
kube-proxy
expl: Cluster Autoscaler watches for pods that cannot be scheduled due to resource shortage and requests more nodes from the cloud provider. HPA = pod replicas; VPA = pod resource requests.

q: CoreDNS in a Kubernetes cluster provides:
opts: Node health monitoring
DNS-based service discovery for pods and services
Ingress load balancing
Pod-to-pod encryption
expl: CoreDNS is the default K8s DNS server. It answers records like my-svc.my-ns.svc.cluster.local so pods can reach Services by name without knowing their ClusterIPs.

q: Which volume type is erased when the pod is removed from a node?
opts: PersistentVolume
emptyDir
hostPath
configMap (mounted)
expl: emptyDir lives and dies with the pod. Useful for scratch space, intermediate processing, or sharing between containers in the same pod. For durability, use PVCs.

q: An admission controller runs:
opts: Before authentication
After authN/authZ, before the request is persisted to etcd
After the pod has started
On every kubectl command client-side
expl: Admission controllers are the last gatekeepers before persistence. They can validate (reject invalid requests) or mutate (inject sidecars, default values). Webhooks let you plug in custom policy.

q: Which is NOT typically a container runtime responsibility?
opts: Pulling images from a registry
Starting and stopping containers
Scheduling pods across nodes
Managing container isolation
expl: Scheduling is kube-scheduler territory (control plane). Runtimes operate at the node level: image mgmt, lifecycle, isolation (via cgroups, namespaces), in cooperation with CNI and CSI plugins.

q: Core principles of the Twelve-Factor App include:
opts: Sharing a single database across all services to keep data consistent
Config in environment variables, backing services as attached resources, stateless processes
Tightly coupling the build and run stages so the same artifact behaves identically
Storing session state in process memory for low-latency access
expl: Twelve-Factor is a SaaS methodology. Key factors: III. Config in env, IV. Backing services as attached resources, VI. Stateless processes, V. Strict separation of build/release/run, XI. Logs as event streams. A violates IV (services should be swappable resources, not a shared substrate). C inverts factor V — build and run must be cleanly separated. D violates VI — processes must be stateless so any instance can serve any request.

q: Serverless (FaaS) means:
opts: A deployment model where developers package and manage their own container runtime
A model where developers don't manage infra; the platform provisions compute on demand and can scale to zero
An architecture where each function runs in its own dedicated VM that stays running
A pattern where application code is executed only on edge devices near the user
expl: Servers still exist — they are just the provider's problem. You pay per execution rather than for idle capacity, and scale-to-zero is a defining property. Examples: AWS Lambda, Knative, OpenFaaS. A inverts the model (developers explicitly do not manage runtime). C describes always-on VM-per-function, which defeats the elasticity and billing model. D describes edge computing, a related but distinct concept.

q: Which CNCF project is a Kubernetes-native serverless platform?
opts: Prometheus
Knative
Envoy
Flux
expl: Knative adds serverless primitives to Kubernetes: autoscaling (including to zero), event sources, and simplified deployment. OpenFaaS is another option in the ecosystem.

q: A service mesh primarily provides:
opts: Image storage
Service-to-service mTLS, observability, and traffic management via sidecar proxies
Cluster autoscaling
GitOps deployments
expl: Meshes (Istio, Linkerd, Consul, Kuma) inject a sidecar proxy (usually Envoy) into each pod. The proxy handles mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, traffic splitting, and emits telemetry — all without app code changes.

q: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) treats operations as:
opts: A role focused exclusively on incident response and on-call rotations
A software engineering discipline that uses error budgets and SLOs to balance reliability and feature velocity
A methodology that merges all developers and operators into a single undifferentiated team
A framework for outsourcing operations to a managed service provider
expl: SRE was formalized at Google. Its defining ideas are SLOs (measurable reliability targets) and error budgets (100% − SLO) — when the budget is burned, feature work pauses until reliability recovers. A is too narrow (SRE is much more than on-call). C describes one cultural variant of DevOps, not SRE specifically. D is the opposite — SRE invests engineering effort internally rather than outsourcing it.

q: A key trade-off of microservices architecture is:
opts: Lower operational complexity than monoliths
Independent deployability, but higher operational complexity (distributed systems challenges)
Guaranteed lower latency
One database for all services
expl: Microservices give teams autonomy but introduce distributed systems pain: network failures, eventual consistency, harder debugging. Do not pick microservices for small teams or simple domains — monoliths are often the right answer.

q: FinOps in a cloud native context refers to:
opts: A practice that brings financial accountability to the variable spend model of cloud, attributing cost to teams and workloads
A CNCF runtime that auto-scales pods up or down based on real-time spot-instance prices
An admission controller that rejects deployments exceeding a namespace's monthly budget
A cost-optimized Kubernetes distribution focused on small businesses
expl: FinOps is a cultural and operational practice (not a piece of software) that brings finance, engineering, and product together to manage cloud spend. In Kubernetes, supporting tools like OpenCost (CNCF) and Kubecost allocate spend back to namespaces, teams, or workloads. B, C, and D are plausible-sounding but invented — there is no such CNCF auto-scaler, no standard budget admission controller, and no FinOps-branded distribution.

q: Which open standard defines the on-disk format and runtime behavior of container images?
opts: OCI (Open Container Initiative)
CNI (Container Network Interface)
CSI (Container Storage Interface)
CRI (Container Runtime Interface)
expl: OCI standardizes the image format (image-spec) and runtime behavior (runtime-spec) so any compliant builder produces images any compliant runtime can run. CNI standardizes pod networking plugins (Calico, Cilium, ...). CSI standardizes storage drivers. CRI is the gRPC interface kubelet uses to talk to runtimes like containerd/CRI-O — it does not define the image format itself.

q: Resilience patterns for distributed systems include:
opts: Synchronous retries with no backoff or jitter
Circuit breakers, exponential backoff with jitter, and graceful degradation
Tight coupling to dependencies so failures fail fast and loudly
Global locks on shared state to serialize all requests
expl: Circuit breakers stop hammering a failing dependency, exponential backoff with jitter prevents thundering-herd retries, and graceful degradation keeps core functionality working when non-critical dependencies fail. A causes retry storms that worsen outages. C amplifies blast radius rather than containing it. D introduces a single point of contention and is an availability anti-pattern in distributed systems.

q: Immutable infrastructure means:
opts: Infrastructure is patched in place after deployment
Components are replaced entirely rather than modified — typically via new images
Infrastructure is tied to specific physical hardware and cannot be moved
Configuration drift is corrected by SSHing in and running scripts
expl: Immutable infrastructure means you replace, not modify. Containers make this natural: build a new image, deploy it, kill the old pod. Benefits: reproducibility, easier rollback, no config drift. A and D both describe mutable approaches (in-place patching, manual remediation); C confuses immutability with hardware coupling.

q: The "three pillars" of observability are:
opts: Logs, Alerts, Dashboards
Metrics, Logs, Traces
CPU, Memory, Disk
Monitoring, Logging, Security
expl: Metrics (numeric time-series), Logs (timestamped events), Traces (request paths through distributed systems). Observability is the goal; these are the signals that enable it.

q: Prometheus is primarily:
opts: A distributed tracing system
A log aggregation system
A time-series database with a pull-based monitoring and alerting model
A service mesh
expl: Prometheus (CNCF graduated) scrapes HTTP metrics endpoints at intervals, stores them in a TSDB, and queries them with PromQL. Alertmanager handles routing. It is the de facto K8s metrics standard.

q: What is OpenTelemetry?
opts: A vendor-neutral observability framework (APIs, SDKs, collectors) for metrics, logs, and traces
A time-series database and metrics backend that replaces Prometheus
A log aggregation agent that ships container stdout to cloud storage
A distributed tracing UI for visualizing service dependency graphs
expl: OpenTelemetry (CNCF) decouples instrumentation from backends: instrument your app once with OTel APIs/SDKs, then route metrics, logs, and traces through the OTel Collector to backends like Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo, or commercial vendors. It is not itself a TSDB (B), a log shipper (C), or a tracing UI (D) — it is the standardized telemetry pipeline that feeds those tools.

q: An SLO (Service Level Objective) is:
opts: An external contract with penalties
An internal target for a service level indicator (e.g., 99.9% success over 30 days)
A current performance measurement
A Grafana dashboard
expl: SLI = what you measure. SLO = target for SLIs (internal). SLA = external contract (usually looser than SLO, with penalties). Error budget = allowable unreliability = 100% − SLO.

q: Which CNCF project is for distributed tracing?
opts: Fluentd
Jaeger
Prometheus
Helm
expl: Jaeger (CNCF graduated, originally from Uber) visualizes how requests flow across services. OpenTelemetry commonly feeds trace data to Jaeger or to Grafana Tempo.

q: GitOps is best described as:
opts: A Git hosting service
An operational pattern where Git is the source of truth and an operator continuously reconciles the cluster
A way to store images in Git
A microservice branch strategy
expl: Git holds desired state. Argo CD or Flux watches the repo and reconciles cluster state to match. Changes happen via pull request. Benefits: auditability, rollback via Git revert, fully declarative.

q: Argo CD and Flux are examples of:
opts: Service mesh implementations
GitOps continuous delivery tools for Kubernetes
Container runtimes
Cluster bootstrap tools
expl: Both are CNCF projects. Argo CD has a rich web UI and app-centric model; Flux is a collection of small controllers composable via the GitOps Toolkit. Either is fine for KCNA.

q: A canary deployment:
opts: Replaces all instances at once in a single cutover
Routes a small share of traffic to the new version, increasing if healthy
Maintains two full environments and switches all traffic at cutover
Replaces pods one at a time without any traffic shaping
expl: Canary = gradual traffic shift with observation. If error rate or latency degrades on the small canary slice, you roll back before most users are affected. A describes a recreate strategy; C describes blue/green (two full environments, atomic switch); D describes a rolling update (instance-by-instance, but every healthy pod gets full traffic share).

q: What is Helm?
opts: A lightweight Kubernetes distribution targeted at edge clusters
A package manager for Kubernetes that uses templated, parameterized charts
A GitOps continuous-delivery operator that reconciles cluster state from a repo
A templating overlay engine for raw YAML that ships built into kubectl
expl: Helm (CNCF graduated) packages Kubernetes manifests into charts with parameterizable values — think apt/brew for Kubernetes. C describes Argo CD or Flux. D describes Kustomize, which uses overlays (not templates) and is invoked via kubectl -k. A is unrelated (e.g., k3s/MicroK8s are distributions, not Helm).

q: What is Tekton?
opts: A service mesh providing mTLS and traffic management between services
A Kubernetes-native CI/CD framework whose pipelines are defined as CRDs and run as pods
A GitOps reconciliation controller that syncs manifests from Git to the cluster
A workflow engine designed primarily for batch and data pipelines
expl: Tekton (CNCF) defines Tasks, Pipelines, and PipelineRuns as Kubernetes CRDs; each step runs in its own pod, making CI/CD a first-class cluster workload. A describes Istio/Linkerd. C describes Argo CD/Flux (GitOps), which is complementary to but distinct from CI. D describes Argo Workflows, which is more general-purpose and commonly used for data/batch jobs rather than build/test/deploy pipelines.

q: Which architectural component ensures that the actual state of cluster resources converges towards the desired state defined in their specifications?
opts: kube-apiserver
kube-scheduler
Controllers (within kube-controller-manager)
kubelet
etcd
expl: Controllers implement reconciliation loops. They watch the desired state via the API server and interact with resources to make the current state match the desired state (e.g., ReplicaSet controller ensuring the right number of Pods exist).

q: A Pod specification includes two containers: one web server and one log shipper. How do these containers communicate efficiently over the network within the Pod?
opts: Via a Kubernetes Service
Via the node's primary IP address
Via localhost and shared network namespace
Via an automatically created NetworkPolicy
Via distinct Pod IP addresses assigned to each container
expl: Containers within the same Pod share the same network namespace, so they can reach each other over localhost on any port — no Service, NetworkPolicy, or extra Pod IP is required. A and B route through cluster networking unnecessarily. D is irrelevant — NetworkPolicies gate traffic, they don't enable it. E is wrong because each Pod gets a single IP shared by all its containers, not one per container.

q: What is the primary role of kubelet on a worker node?
opts: To schedule Pods onto the node
To store the state of Pods running on the node
To ensure containers described in PodSpecs assigned to its node are running
To manage network routing rules for Services
To authenticate API requests originating from the node
expl: The kubelet is the primary node agent. It receives PodSpecs from the API server and interacts with the container runtime (e.g., containerd) to start, stop, and manage the containers defined in those Pods, reporting their status back to the control plane.

q: Which Kubernetes resource provides a mechanism for grouping API objects and providing a scope for names?
opts: Label
Annotation
Namespace
Deployment
ServiceAccount
expl: Namespaces create logical partitions within a cluster, allowing teams or applications to operate independently. Resource names must be unique within a Namespace, but not necessarily across the entire cluster.

q: When interacting with the Kubernetes API server using kubectl, what is the typical format used for defining resource manifests?
opts: JSON
XML
YAML
Protocol Buffers
Plain Text
expl: While the API server can accept JSON, YAML is the overwhelmingly conventional and human-readable format used in configuration files (manifests) passed to kubectl for creating or updating resources.

q: What core Kubernetes concept allows Deployments to perform rolling updates with zero downtime?
opts: Managing StatefulSets
Utilizing PersistentVolumes
Incrementally replacing Pods managed by ReplicaSets
Direct communication with the kube-scheduler
Modifying NetworkPolicy rules dynamically
expl: A Deployment performs rolling updates by creating a new ReplicaSet and gradually scaling it up while scaling down the old ReplicaSet. Pods are replaced incrementally according to maxSurge and maxUnavailable settings, so a healthy set of replicas keeps serving traffic throughout the rollout, achieving zero downtime.

q: How does Kubernetes typically handle the failure of a container within a Pod?
opts: By deleting the entire Pod immediately
By restarting the failed container based on the Pod's restartPolicy
By scheduling a new Pod on a different node
By marking the Node as unschedulable
By alerting the kube-scheduler to find a replacement container
expl: The kubelet monitors container health. If a container fails, the kubelet restarts it according to the Pod's restartPolicy (Always, OnFailure, Never). The Pod itself continues to exist on the same node.

q: What distinguishes a Kubernetes Secret from a ConfigMap?
opts: Secrets are namespaced, ConfigMaps are not
ConfigMaps store configuration data, Secrets store sensitive data (base64 encoded)
Secrets can only be mounted as environment variables, ConfigMaps as files
ConfigMaps are automatically encrypted at rest, Secrets are not
Secrets are immutable once created, ConfigMaps are mutable
expl: Both are used for configuration, but Secrets are intended for sensitive information (passwords, keys) and are stored base64 encoded (not truly encrypted by default in etcd, though). ConfigMaps are for nonsensitive configuration data. Both are namespaced and can be mounted in multiple ways.

q: Which of the following is NOT a standard component of the Kubernetes control plane?
opts: etcd
kube-apiserver
kube-scheduler
containerd
kube-controller-manager
expl: containerd is a container runtime; it typically runs on worker nodes (managed by the kubelet) to handle container execution, not as part of the central control plane.

q: What is the purpose of labels in Kubernetes?
opts: To provide detailed descriptive information about a resource
To define network access rules between resources
To attach identifying metadata for selection and organization
To specify resource requests and limits for containers
To store multi-line configuration data
expl: Labels are key/value pairs attached to objects (like Pods, Services) used to organize resources and allow users/controllers to select subsets of objects (e.g., a Service selecting Pods with app=backend).

q: When defining a Pod, what does the spec.containers[].image field specify?
opts: The base operating system for the container
The specific container image (and tag) to run
The command to execute inside the container
The resource limits for the container
The network port the container will listen on
expl: This field tells the container runtime which image (e.g., nginx:1.21 or myrepo/myapp:v2.5) to pull from a registry and use to create the container process.

q: If a Pod needs access to specific hardware features available only on certain nodes, which scheduling mechanism is most suitable?
opts: ResourceQuota
NetworkPolicy
PodDisruptionBudget
Taints and Tolerations or Node Affinity
HorizontalPodAutoscaler
expl: Taints applied to nodes repel Pods that don't Tolerate them. Node Affinity allows Pods to express preferences or requirements for nodes based on node labels (which can represent hardware features, location, etc.). Both help ensure Pods land on appropriate nodes.

q: Which API object represents a single point of access to a set of Pods providing the same functionality, acting as an internal load balancer?
opts: Ingress
EndpointSlice
Service
ReplicaSet
NetworkPolicy
expl: A Service defines a logical set of Pods (usually selected by labels) and provides a stable IP address and DNS name. kube-proxy ensures traffic to the Service IP is load-balanced across the healthy backend Pods.

q: What is the relationship between a Deployment and a ReplicaSet?
opts: A ReplicaSet manages multiple Deployments
A Deployment watches ReplicaSets for scaling signals
A Deployment declaratively manages ReplicaSets to orchestrate Pod updates
ReplicaSets are used only for stateful applications, Deployments for stateless
They are independent controllers managing Pod lifecycles
expl: A Deployment provides declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets. It creates and manages ReplicaSets, handling rolling updates or rollbacks by manipulating the desired replica count of underlying ReplicaSets. You typically interact with Deployments, not ReplicaSets directly.

q: The Kubernetes API server performs several actions when receiving a request. Which action verifies if the authenticated user is allowed to perform the requested operation?
opts: Authentication
Admission Control
Authorization
Rate Limiting
Validation
expl: After Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (e.g., via RBAC) checks if the identified user has the necessary permissions (Role/ClusterRole bindings) to perform the action (e.g., create, get, delete) on the specific resource (e.g., pods) in the target namespace.

q: What is the primary function of etcd within the Kubernetes architecture?
opts: To run the container runtime interface
To schedule Pods across available nodes
To provide reliable, distributed storage for cluster state and configuration
To manage network policies and ingress rules
To execute health checks against running containers
expl: etcd is the consistent key-value store that acts as the source of truth for the entire cluster. All configuration, state, and metadata are persisted here, ensuring consistency across the control plane.

q: Which field in a Pod's specification is crucial for the kubescheduler to determine if a node has sufficient resources?
opts: spec.nodeName
spec.containers[].ports
spec.containers[].resources.requests
spec.serviceAccountName
spec.restartPolicy
expl: The resources.requests field (for CPU and memory) indicates the minimum amount of resources the container needs. The scheduler uses this to filter out nodes that cannot meet these requests.

q: What fundamental concept allows Kubernetes controllers to operate effectively without needing constant instructions?
opts: Imperative command execution
The reconciliation loop (Control Loop)
Direct manipulation of etcd
Event-driven webhooks
Stateful session management
expl: Controllers continuously watch the desired state (from the API server) and the current state (observed in the cluster), taking actions (via the API server) to make the current state match the desired state. This is the core principle of Kubernetes automation.

q: Which OCI (Open Container Initiative) specification defines how container runtimes should execute containers?
opts: Image Specification
Distribution Specification
Runtime Specification
Network Specification
Storage Specification
expl: The OCI Runtime Specification outlines how to run a container filesystem bundle. Kubernetes uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) as an abstraction layer over runtimes like containerd or CRI-O that implement this standard.

q: You need to provide non-sensitive configuration data (like a URL endpoint or logging level) to a Pod. Which resource is most appropriate?
opts: Secret
ConfigMap
Annotation
ResourceQuota
DownwardAPI
expl: A ConfigMap stores non-sensitive configuration data as key/value pairs. It can be consumed by Pods as environment variables, command-line arguments, or files mounted into a volume, decoupling configuration from container images. Secrets serve the same role for sensitive data; ConfigMaps are the right choice for plain values like URLs or log levels.

q: What does it mean for the Kubernetes API to be "declarative"?
opts: API calls must be made sequentially
Users specify the desired end state, not the steps to reach it
The API only accepts YAML formatted requests
API responses always include the full resource specification
The API can only create resources, not update or delete them
expl: Users declare the desired state of resources (e.g., "I want 3 replicas of this Pod"). Kubernetes controllers then figure out the sequence of actions needed to achieve and maintain that state. Contrast this with imperative approaches ("run container X", "scale up by 1").

q: Which mechanism prevents the kube-scheduler from placing Pods on a Node that is undergoing maintenance or is otherwise unsuitable?
opts: ResourceQuotas
NetworkPolicies
Taints on the Node and Tolerations on the Pod
PodDisruptionBudgets
PriorityClasses
expl: A Taint applied to a Node repels Pods. Pods can express Tolerations for specific taints, allowing them to be scheduled onto those nodes. This is commonly used to cordon nodes for maintenance or dedicate nodes for specific workloads.

q: Which component translates a Service's virtual IP address into actual Pod IP addresses on each node?
opts: etcd
kube-apiserver
coredns / kube-dns
kube-proxy
kubelet
expl: kube-proxy, running on each node, watches Services and Endpoints/EndpointSlices. It programs network rules (e.g., iptables, IPVS) to intercept traffic destined for a Service's ClusterIP and directs it to one of the healthy backend Pod IPs.

q: A StatefulSet is often preferred over a Deployment for running databases. Why?
opts: StatefulSets have simpler configuration
Deployments cannot use Persistent Storage
StatefulSets provide stable, unique network IDs and persistent storage per Pod
Deployments automatically handle database schema migrations
StatefulSets require less CPU and memory resources
expl: StatefulSets provide guarantees crucial for stateful apps: stable network identifiers (e.g., db-0, db-1), stable persistent storage linked to each identity, and ordered, graceful deployment and scaling.

q: What is the primary goal of the Open Container Initiative (OCI)?
opts: To create a commercial container platform
To define open standards around container formats and runtime
To manage the Kubernetes source code repository
To develop container networking plugins (CNI)
To provide free container image hosting
expl: OCI aims to establish common, open, minimal standards for container image formats and container runtime execution to ensure interoperability and prevent fragmentation in the container ecosystem.

q: Which kubectl command is used to view the logs of a running container within a Pod?
opts: kubectl get pod <pod-name> --logs
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
kubectl logs <pod-name> [-c <container-name>]
kubectl attach <pod-name> -c <container-name>
kubectl exec <pod-name> -- tail /var/log/messages
expl: kubectl logs fetches the logs (stdout/stderr) from a specified container within a Pod. The -c flag is needed if the Pod has multiple containers.

q: What is the function of an Admission Controller in the Kubernetes API request lifecycle?
opts: To authenticate the user making the request
To authorize the requested operation
To mutate or validate API objects before they are persisted in etcd
To schedule the Pod onto a suitable node
To route network traffic to the correct Pod
expl: Admission Controllers intercept API requests after authentication and authorization but before persistence. They can mutate objects (e.g., inject sidecars, set default values) or validate them (e.g., enforce security policies, check resource quotas).

q: Which resource defines constraints on the total amount of compute resources (CPU, memory) that can be consumed within a specific Namespace?
opts: LimitRange
ResourceQuota
NetworkPolicy
PodSecurityPolicy / PodSecurityAdmission
PriorityClass
expl: A ResourceQuota limits the aggregate resource consumption (CPU, memory, storage, object counts) within a Namespace, preventing overuse and ensuring fair sharing among users or teams.

q: Consider a Pod that needs to perform a one-time initialization task before its main application container starts. Which container type is best suited for this?
opts: Sidecar Container
Ephemeral Container
Init Container
Main Application Container
Job Container
expl: Init Containers run sequentially before the main application containers in a Pod start. They must complete successfully for the main containers to launch, making them ideal for setup tasks like database schema migration, fetching configuration, or waiting for dependencies.

q: What is the purpose of the metadata.ownerReferences field in a Kubernetes object?
opts: To specify the user who created the object
To link the object to its managing controller (e.g., ReplicaSet to Deployment)
To list labels used for selecting the object
To store annotations related to the object's owner
To define the Namespace the object belongs to
expl: ownerReferences create parent-child relationships between objects. This enables cascading deletion (deleting the owner deletes dependents) and helps controllers identify the objects they manage. For example, a ReplicaSet Pod will have an ownerReference pointing to the ReplicaSet.

q: Which statement best describes the relationship between Kubernetes and Docker (as of 2025)?
opts: Kubernetes requires Docker to be installed on all nodes.
Docker is the only container runtime supported by Kubernetes.
Kubernetes primarily interacts with container runtimes via CRI (e.g., containerd, CRI-O), abstracting Docker.
Docker manages the Kubernetes control plane components.
Kubernetes is a component within the Docker Enterprise platform.
expl: Kubernetes deprecated the direct Docker integration (dockershim). It now interacts with OCI-compliant runtimes like containerd (which Docker itself uses) or CRI-O via the Container Runtime Interface (CRI). While Docker might be installed, K8s doesn't use it directly in the same way.

q: If multiple Pods need to share persistent data, which volume type allows simultaneous mounting by multiple Pods (potentially with read/write access)?
opts: hostPath
emptyDir
A PersistentVolume with an accessMode like ReadWriteMany (RWX)
secret volume
configMap volume
expl: PersistentVolumes (PVs) represent external storage. Their accessModes define how they can be mounted. ReadWriteOnce (RWO) allows mounting by a single node, while ReadWriteMany (RWX) allows simultaneous read/write mounting by multiple nodes (requires a capable storage backend like NFS).

q: Which control plane component is primarily responsible for running controllers like the Node Controller, Deployment Controller, and Service Controller?
opts: etcd
kube-apiserver
kube-scheduler
kube-controller-manager
cloud-controller-manager
expl: The kube-controller-manager bundles core Kubernetes controllers into a single binary. It runs controllers that handle node lifecycle, workload management (Deployments, ReplicaSets), service endpoints, namespace creation, etc.

q: What is the purpose of a LimitRange object within a Namespace?
opts: To limit the total resource usage of the entire Namespace
To set default resource requests/limits for containers and validate min/max bounds
To restrict network traffic between Pods in the Namespace
To control which users can create resources in the Namespace
To define storage quotas for PersistentVolumeClaims
expl: A LimitRange operates at the container/Pod level within a Namespace. It can enforce minimum/maximum resource constraints and specify default request/limit values if they aren't explicitly set in the Pod spec. ResourceQuota limits the Namespace total.

q: Which kubectl command would you use to apply a configuration defined in my-app.yaml declaratively?
opts: kubectl create -f my-app.yaml
kubectl replace -f my-app.yaml
kubectl apply -f my-app.yaml
kubectl patch -f my-app.yaml
kubectl run -f my-app.yaml
expl: kubectl apply is the standard command for declarative resource management. It creates resources if they don't exist or calculates and applies necessary changes if they do, based on the definition in the file.

q: How does the kube-scheduler handle Pods with defined affinity rules?
opts: It ignores affinity rules if resource requests cannot be met.
It uses affinity rules as the primary factor for node selection.
It considers affinity/anti-affinity rules during the filtering and scoring phases.
It delegates affinity processing to the kubelet.
It only processes podAffinity, not nodeAffinity.
expl: Affinity rules (node and inter-pod) influence scheduling decisions. Required affinity rules act as filters (nodes must match). Preferred affinity rules contribute to the scoring phase (nodes matching preferred rules get higher scores).

q: What is the concept of "desired state reconciliation" fundamental to Kubernetes?
opts: Users manually reconcile cluster state using kubectl.
Controllers continuously observe and work to match actual state to declared state.
etcd automatically corrects discrepancies between desired and actual state.
kubelet reconciles container state based solely on node health.
The API server rejects any configuration that deviates from the current state.
expl: This is the core operational principle. Users declare the desired state (via API objects), and various controllers run loops comparing this desired state with the actual observed state, taking corrective actions (creating/deleting/updating resources) to close the gap.

q: Which resource attribute is primarily used by a Service to determine which Pods should receive traffic?
opts: metadata.name
metadata.namespace
metadata.labels
spec.containers[].image
spec.serviceAccountName
expl: A Service defines a label selector in its spec. The endpoints controller continuously watches Pods whose metadata.labels match that selector and populates the Service's Endpoints/EndpointSlices, which kube-proxy then uses to route traffic. Names, namespaces, images, and service accounts play no role in target selection.

q: What type of container volume shares the Pod's lifecycle and is initially empty, useful for temporary data sharing between containers in a Pod?
opts: hostPath
persistentVolumeClaim
emptyDir
nfs
secret
expl: An emptyDir volume is created when a Pod is assigned to a node and exists for as long as that Pod runs there. Its contents are lost when the Pod is removed from the node. Because all containers in the Pod can mount it at the same path, it is ideal as scratch space or for sharing files between containers within a single Pod.

q: Which Kubernetes API endpoint would kubectl get pods likely interact with?
opts: /api/v1/nodes
/api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods
/apis/apps/v1/deployments
/healthz
/metrics
expl: kubectl translates resource types into REST paths against the Kubernetes API server. Pods are part of the core API group (no group name), so they live under /api/v1, and because Pods are namespaced the full path is /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods. Deployments belong to the apps group (/apis/apps/v1/...), while /healthz and /metrics are server-level endpoints, not resource collections.

q: Which architectural pattern describes running a helper container alongside a main application container in the same Pod to provide auxiliary functionality?
opts: Init Container
Ephemeral Container
Sidecar Container
Ambassador Container
Adapter Container
expl: The Sidecar pattern involves adding containers to a Pod to extend or enhance the main application container (e.g., for logging, monitoring, proxying) without modifying the main application's code. They share the same lifecycle and network/storage namespaces.

q: How are API resources like Deployments and StatefulSets grouped in the Kubernetes API?
opts: Under the core v1 API group (/api/v1)
They are not part of any API group.
Under specific API groups like apps/v1
Under the scheduling.k8s.io API group
Under the batch/v1 API group
expl: Kubernetes organizes its API into groups to facilitate evolution. Workload resources like Deployments, StatefulSets, ReplicaSets, and DaemonSets are part of the apps API group, currently at version v1.

q: If a Node fails, what component detects this failure and updates the Node's status in etcd?
opts: kubelet on the failed node
kube-proxy on peer nodes
node-controller (within kube-controller-manager)
kube-scheduler
etcd itself through distributed consensus
expl: The node-controller, which runs as part of the kube-controller-manager, is responsible for monitoring Node health based on heartbeats received from each kubelet. When heartbeats stop arriving, it updates the Node's status conditions (e.g., Ready becomes False or Unknown), which is then persisted to etcd via the API server.

q: Which resource configuration allows a Pod to securely access the Kubernetes API server using the Pod's own identity?
opts: Mounting a Secret containing API keys
Configuring a ServiceAccount for the Pod and mounting its token
Using hostNetwork: true
Defining an Ingress resource
Setting environment variables with kubeconfig data
expl: A ServiceAccount provides an identity for a Pod. Kubernetes automatically creates and mounts a token for this ServiceAccount into the Pod (unless disabled), which applications can use to authenticate to the API server. Permissions are granted via RBAC.

q: What is the primary purpose of using annotations on Kubernetes resources?
opts: To select resources for operations (like Services selecting Pods)
To define resource constraints and quotas
To attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata (often used by tools)
To specify the desired number of replicas for a workload
To enforce security policies on Pods
expl: While labels are for identifying and selecting objects, annotations are meant for attaching non-identifying metadata, often used by tools, libraries, or operators to store configuration, pointers, or state information relevant to their function.

q: In a typical Kubernetes cluster setup, where does the kubescheduler run?
opts: On every worker node
As a Pod managed by a DaemonSet
As a static Pod or systemd service on a control plane node
Inside the etcd cluster
As part of the kubelet binary
expl: The kube-scheduler is a critical control plane component. It usually runs directly on a control plane node, often managed as a static Pod (defined by a manifest file read by the kubelet on the control plane node) or as a system service.

q: What is a primary motivation for adopting container orchestration beyond simply running containers with a runtime like containerd?
opts: To achieve higher density of containers on a single host.
To automate deployment, scaling, healing, and networking of distributed applications.
To simplify the process of building container images from source code.
To provide a standardized container image format for portability.
To enforce strict kernel isolation between containers.
expl: While runtimes manage container lifecycle on a single host, orchestration automates complex tasks across a cluster of hosts, such as declarative deployments, auto-scaling based on load, self-healing of failed instances, service discovery, and load balancing.

q: The Container Runtime Interface (CRI) in Kubernetes serves what key purpose?
opts: To define the standard for container image formats.
To provide an API for kubelet to interact with different container runtimes.
To manage network plugin configurations (CNI).
To enforce security policies for container execution.
To schedule containers onto nodes.
expl: CRI is a plugin interface enabling kubelet to use various container runtimes (like containerd, CRI-O) without recompiling Kubernetes. It standardizes communication for operations like starting/stopping Pods and containers.

q: In Kubernetes RBAC, what is the difference between a Role and a ClusterRole?
opts: Role grants permissions to users, ClusterRole to ServiceAccounts.
Role is for read-only access, ClusterRole for write access.
Role is namespaced, ClusterRole is cluster-wide.
Role defines permissions, ClusterRole binds permissions to subjects.
Role is for built-in components, ClusterRole for custom resources.
expl: Role objects define permissions within a specific namespace, while ClusterRole objects define permissions that apply cluster-wide (e.g., to access nodes, persistent volumes, or resources across all namespaces).

q: Which Kubernetes networking component is responsible for ensuring that traffic sent to a Service's IP address is correctly routed to one of its backing Pods?
opts: CNI plugin
kube-dns / CoreDNS
kube-proxy
Ingress controller
The API server's endpoint controller.
expl: kube-proxy runs on each node and maintains network rules (e.g., using iptables, IPVS) that implement Kubernetes Services. It watches the API server for Service and EndpointSlice changes and updates these rules accordingly.

q: What fundamental problem does a Service Mesh aim to solve for microservices that is not inherently addressed by basic Kubernetes Services?
opts: Providing stable IP addresses for Pods.
Exposing services to external traffic via HTTP/S routing.
Adding advanced observability, security (mTLS), and traffic control between services.
Managing persistent storage for stateful applications.
Automating the build and deployment of container images.
expl: While Kubernetes Services provide basic L4 load balancing and service discovery, a Service Mesh (like Istio, Linkerd) adds a dedicated infrastructure layer for L7 concerns like fine-grained traffic management (canary, retries), mutual TLS (mTLS) between services, and detailed telemetry (metrics, traces).

q: What is the role of a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) in Kubernetes storage?
opts: It defines a specific type of storage backend (e.g., SSD, HDD).
It represents a piece of provisioned storage available in the cluster.
It is a request for storage by a user/Pod, consuming a PersistentVolume.
It directly attaches a node's local disk to a Pod.
It manages the lifecycle of storage snapshots.
expl: A PVC is a request for storage by a user. It consumes an existing PersistentVolume (PV) or can dynamically provision one if a StorageClass is configured. Pods then mount the PVC as a volume.

q: How does container orchestration typically facilitate "self-healing" for applications?
opts: By automatically applying security patches to container images.
By using AI to predict and prevent application failures.
By monitoring application health and automatically restarting or replacing failed instances.
By providing detailed debugging tools for developers to fix application bugs faster.
By load balancing traffic away from nodes with high CPU usage.
expl: Orchestrators monitor the health of application instances (e.g., Pods). If an instance fails a health check or crashes, the orchestrator can automatically restart it or replace it with a new instance to maintain the desired state and availability.

q: Which of these is a direct responsibility of an OCI-compliant container runtime like containerd?
opts: Scheduling containers across a cluster of machines.
Managing the lifecycle of containers on a single host (create, start, stop, delete).
Defining the desired state of application deployments.
Providing inter-service authentication using mTLS.
Allocating persistent storage volumes from cloud providers.
expl: Container runtimes like containerd are responsible for managing the complete container lifecycle on a single node, including pulling images, creating, starting, stopping, and deleting containers as instructed by a higher-level component like the kubelet.

q: What is the primary purpose of a ServiceAccount in Kubernetes security?
opts: To provide an identity for human users to log into the cluster.
To define permissions for accessing Kubernetes API resources.
To provide a distinct identity for processes running inside Pods to interact with the API.
To securely store API tokens and certificates for external services.
To encrypt network traffic between Pods.
expl: ServiceAccounts are meant for processes within Pods, giving them an identity that can be authenticated by the API server and authorized via RBAC to perform specific actions. Human users typically use regular user accounts or external identity providers.

q: What role does the Container Network Interface (CNI) play in Kubernetes networking?
opts: It defines the API for how kube-proxy implements Services.
It provides a specification for writing plugins to configure network interfaces for Pods.
It manages DNS resolution for Services and Pods within the cluster.
It enforces network policies to restrict traffic flow between Pods.
It provides a secure tunnel for control plane communication.
expl: CNI is a specification and a set of libraries for writing plugins to configure network interfaces in Linux containers. In Kubernetes, CNI plugins are responsible for connecting Pods to the cluster network and assigning IP addresses.

q: Which feature is typically NOT offered as a core capability by a Service Mesh?
opts: Automatic mTLS encryption between services.
Fine-grained traffic routing (e.g., canary deployments, A/B testing).
Distributed tracing and observability metrics for inter-service communication.
Container image vulnerability scanning.
Resiliency features like retries and timeouts.
expl: Service Meshes focus on managing and securing interservice communication (runtime concerns). Image vulnerability scanning is typically part of the CI/CD pipeline or a separate security tool, addressing build-time or registry concerns.

q: What is the primary function of a StorageClass in Kubernetes?
opts: To directly provide storage to a Pod.
To define different "classes" or types of storage for dynamic provisioning.
To claim a specific PersistentVolume for use.
To backup and restore data from PersistentVolumes.
To limit the amount of storage a namespace can consume.
expl: StorageClasses allow administrators to define different types of storage (e.g., "fast-ssd", "cheap-hdd", "backup-storage") with specific provisioners and parameters. This enables dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim requests a certain class.

q: How does declarative configuration, a common feature in container orchestration, improve system reliability compared to imperative commands?
opts: It executes commands faster.
It allows users to specify the exact sequence of operations for desired outcomes.
It describes the desired state, allowing the orchestrator to continuously reconcile.
It requires less YAML or JSON to define resources.
It automatically selects the most performant hardware.
expl: With declarative configuration, users define the desired end state. The orchestrator then works to achieve and maintain this state, automatically correcting drift. This is more robust than imperative commands which specify how to do something and don't inherently handle drift.

q: What is the significance of the dockershim component being removed from Kubernetes starting from v1.24?
opts: Kubernetes no longer supports Docker-formatted container images.
Developers must use a different command-line tool instead of docker build.
Kubernetes now directly uses container runtimes that implement CRI, like containerd.
All existing Docker containers must be rebuilt using a new OCI tool.
Pods can no longer run multiple containers.
expl: The removal of dockershim means Kubernetes no longer has built-in direct support for the Docker Engine as a runtime. Instead, it relies on runtimes that implement the Container Runtime Interface (CRI), such as containerd (which Docker itself uses) or CRI-O. Docker images still work.

q: When a NetworkPolicy in Kubernetes selects a Pod, what does it primarily control?
opts: The Pod's ability to access external services outside the cluster.
The amount of network bandwidth the Pod can consume.
The ingress and egress network traffic for that Pod at L3/L4.
The DNS resolution behavior for the Pod.
The encryption of data in transit for the Pod.
expl: A NetworkPolicy is a namespaced resource that selects Pods via a podSelector and defines which ingress and egress traffic is allowed at L3/L4 (IP addresses/CIDRs, ports, and protocols). The policy itself is just a specification; the cluster's CNI plugin (e.g., Calico, Cilium) is responsible for enforcing it at the network layer.

q: If a company wants to implement zero-trust security for interservice communication within their Kubernetes cluster, what would a Service Mesh primarily contribute?
opts: Encrypting persistent data at rest.
Enforcing strong authentication and authorization for kubectl users.
Providing automatic mutual TLS (mTLS) between all services in the mesh.
Scanning Pods for known vulnerabilities.
Managing firewall rules at the cluster's edge.
expl: A key feature of many Service Meshes is the ability to automatically establish and enforce mTLS for all traffic between services in the mesh. This encrypts traffic and ensures that services mutually authenticate each other, a core tenet of zero-trust networking.

q: The Container Storage Interface (CSI) was developed to address what challenge in Kubernetes storage?
opts: To provide a standard for defining StorageClass parameters.
To enable third-party storage vendors to develop plugins without modifying core Kubernetes code.
To improve the performance of hostPath volumes.
To automate the backup and restore of PersistentVolumes.
To define how containers should access configuration data.
expl: CSI is a standard for exposing block and file storage systems to containerized workloads. It allows storage vendors to create plugins that integrate with Kubernetes (and other orchestrators) to manage storage lifecycles, without needing their code to be part of the Kubernetes core.

q: Which of these is NOT a typical benefit of using a container orchestrator for managing applications?
opts: Improved resource utilization through bin packing.
Simplified application code logic for handling infrastructure failures.
Abstracted underlying infrastructure differences.
Automatic conversion of monolithic applications to microservices.
Enhanced scalability and availability of applications.
expl: Orchestrators help deploy and manage microservices (and even well-architected monoliths), but they do not automatically refactor or convert application code from a monolithic architecture to a microservices architecture. That's a software design and development effort.

q: What is a key characteristic of runc, often used by higher-level runtimes like containerd?
opts: It's a full-fledged container orchestrator.
It provides a command-line tool for building OCI-compliant images.
It's a low-level OCI runtime responsible for spawning and running containers.
It's a CNI plugin for configuring Pod networking.
It's a distributed key-value store for container metadata.
expl: runc is a CLI tool for spawning and running containers according to the OCI specification. Higher-level runtimes like containerd use runc (or similar OCI runtimes) under the hood to actually execute the containers.

q: What is the primary purpose of Secrets in Kubernetes, and how are they typically made available to Pods?
opts: To store arbitrary non-sensitive configuration; as environment variables or files.
To store sensitive data (e.g., passwords, tokens); as environment variables or volume mounts.
To define network policies; applied directly by kubelet.
To store TLS certificates for Ingress; only used by Ingress controllers.
To store container image pull credentials; only used by kubelet.
expl: Secrets are designed for sensitive information. While they can be used for image pull credentials, their broader use is for application secrets. They are base64 encoded (not encrypted by default in etcd) and can be mounted as files into Pods or exposed as environment variables.

q: An Ingress resource in Kubernetes provides what type of functionality?
opts: L4 TCP/UDP load balancing for internal services.
Management of external HTTP/S access to services, with host/path based routing.
Network isolation between Pods within the same namespace.
Dynamic provisioning of persistent storage for web servers.
A CNI plugin implementation for advanced networking features.
expl: Ingress objects manage external access to in-cluster Services, typically over HTTP and HTTPS. They provide L7 features such as host-based routing (e.g., foo.example.com) and path-based routing (e.g., example.com/bar), along with TLS termination. An Ingress controller (such as NGINX, Traefik, or a cloud provider's controller) actually implements the routing rules defined by Ingress resources.

q: In the context of a Service Mesh, what does "traffic splitting" commonly refer to?
opts: Dividing network bandwidth equally among all services.
Routing a percentage of traffic to different versions of a service (e.g., for canary).
Encrypting only a portion of the traffic between services.
Splitting large data packets into smaller frames for transmission.
Blocking traffic from specific IP address ranges.
expl: Traffic splitting is a Service Mesh capability that allows routing specific percentages of incoming requests to different versions of a service. This is crucial for deployment strategies like canary releases or A/B testing, allowing gradual rollouts and risk mitigation.

q: If a Pod needs temporary scratch space that is empty upon creation and deleted when the Pod terminates, which volume type is most appropriate?
opts: hostPath
persistentVolumeClaim
emptyDir
configMap
nfs
expl: An emptyDir volume is created when a Pod is assigned to a node. It is initially empty, and its contents are deleted when the Pod is removed from the node. It's useful for scratch space or sharing files between containers in the same Pod.

q: What is a key security implication of running containers as a non-root user?
opts: It prevents containers from accessing network resources.
It reduces the potential impact if the container process is compromised.
It allows containers to bypass RBAC checks for API access.
It disables the container runtime's ability to pull images from private registries.
It encrypts all data written by the container to its filesystem.
expl: Running container processes as a non-root user is a security best practice. If an attacker compromises the process, they gain the privileges of that non-root user, which are significantly less than root, limiting their ability to harm the host system or other containers.

q: What is the typical interaction flow when kubelet needs to start a Pod using a CRI-compliant runtime?
opts: kubelet directly calls runc to create the container.
kubelet sends a request to the CRI gRPC server implemented by the runtime.
kubelet updates etcd, and the runtime reads the changes.
kubelet instructs kube-proxy to prepare the network for the runtime.
kubelet uses kubectl commands to tell the runtime what to do.
expl: kubelet acts as a client to the CRI gRPC server implemented by the container runtime (e.g., containerd). It sends requests like RunPodSandbox (to create the Pod's environment) and CreateContainer/StartContainer for each container in the Pod.

q: In Kubernetes, what is the purpose of an EndpointSlice object in relation to a Service?
opts: To define the external IP address for a LoadBalancer Service.
To store the DNS configuration for a Service.
To efficiently track and provide the IP addresses and ports of Pods backing a Service.
To enforce network policies for traffic destined to a Service.
To manage TLS termination for a Service.
expl: EndpointSlices offer a more scalable and extensible way to track the network endpoints (Pod IPs and ports) that a Service routes to. They replaced the older Endpoints object for improved performance, especially in large clusters with many Pods per Service.

q: Which component of a Service Mesh architecture is typically injected as a sidecar container into application Pods?
opts: The control plane's central policy manager.
The data plane proxy (e.g., Envoy, Linkerd2-proxy).
The certificate authority for issuing mTLS certificates.
The metrics collection and aggregation server.
The UI dashboard for visualizing mesh topology.
expl: In a Service Mesh, a data plane proxy (like Envoy or Linkerd2-proxy) is usually deployed as a sidecar container alongside each application container in the Pod. This proxy intercepts all network traffic to and from the application container, enabling the mesh's features.

q: A StatefulSet requires stable, unique network identifiers for its Pods. How is this typically achieved in Kubernetes?
opts: By manually assigning static IP addresses to each Pod.
Through a headless Service that creates DNS records for each Pod.
Using hostNetwork: true for all Pods in the StatefulSet.
By relying on CNI plugins to assign predictable IPs from a predefined range.
By using NetworkPolicy to restrict Pod IPs.
expl: StatefulSets often use a headless Service (a Service with clusterIP: None). This causes DNS to create A records for each Pod (e.g., mypod-0.my-service.my-namespace) that resolve directly to the Pod's IP, providing stable, resolvable hostnames.

q: When using dynamic provisioning for Kubernetes storage, what happens if a PersistentVolumeClaim requests a StorageClass that does not exist?
opts: The PVC remains in a Pending state indefinitely or until the class is created.
Kubernetes automatically creates a default StorageClass and uses it.
The Pod attempting to use the PVC will fail to start with a network error.
The PVC will use any available PersistentVolume regardless of its StorageClass.
The request defaults to using hostPath storage on the assigned node.
expl: If a PVC specifies a StorageClass that doesn't exist, and no default StorageClass is able to fulfill the request, the PVC will remain in the Pending state because no storage can be dynamically provisioned to satisfy its requirements.

q: What is the primary benefit of using orchestration for rolling updates compared to manual, script-based updates?
opts: Faster individual container restart times.
Guaranteed zero cost for update operations.
Automated health checks, progressive rollout, and easier rollback capabilities.
Elimination of the need for container image registries.
In-place binary patching of running application code.
expl: Orchestrators manage rolling updates by incrementally replacing old instances with new ones, performing health checks on new instances before proceeding, and allowing for automated or easy rollbacks if issues arise. This is far more robust than manual scripting.

q: What is a key difference between containerd and Docker Engine in the context of Kubernetes (post-dockershim removal)?
opts: containerd cannot run Docker-formatted images; Docker Engine can.
Docker Engine includes build tools and a CLI; containerd is a core runtime.
containerd is not OCI compliant; Docker Engine is.
Docker Engine implements CRI directly; containerd requires a shim.
containerd is only for Linux; Docker Engine is cross-platform.
expl: Docker Engine is a larger platform that includes client tools (docker CLI), image building capabilities, and the containerd runtime itself. containerd is a focused OCI-compliant core runtime component designed to be embedded into larger systems like Kubernetes or Docker Engine.

q: How does Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) in Kubernetes contribute to the principle of least privilege?
opts: By encrypting all API communication by default.
By ensuring all Pods run with minimal resource requests and limits.
By allowing administrators to grant users/ServiceAccounts only necessary permissions.
By automatically rotating credentials for ServiceAccounts.
By restricting network access between namespaces.
expl: RBAC lets cluster administrators define Roles and ClusterRoles that list exactly which verbs (get, list, create, etc.) are allowed on which resources, then bind those roles to specific users, groups, or ServiceAccounts via RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings. By granting only the permissions a subject actually needs, RBAC directly enables the principle of least privilege.

q: What is the concept of a "control plane" in a Service Mesh architecture?
opts: The set of sidecar proxies running alongside application containers.
The central components that manage and configure the data plane proxies.
The application code responsible for handling business logic.
The underlying Kubernetes cluster nodes.
The network infrastructure connecting the cluster to the internet.
expl: The Service Mesh control plane (e.g., Istiod in Istio) provides the management functions. It configures the data plane proxies (sidecars) with policies, collects telemetry from them, and manages service discovery and certificate rotation within the mesh.

q: If a PersistentVolume is reclaimed using the Delete reclaim policy, what happens to the underlying storage when the associated PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted?
opts: The storage is archived and can be restored later.
The PersistentVolume object is deleted, but the data on the storage medium remains.
The data on the underlying storage medium is deleted.
The PersistentVolume becomes available for another PersistentVolumeClaim.
The storage is automatically resized to its minimum capacity.
expl: With the Delete reclaim policy, deleting the PVC will also trigger the deletion of the PersistentVolume object and the associated storage asset in the external infrastructure (e.g., AWS EBS volume, GCE PD). The Retain policy keeps the data.

q: A PodSecurityContext can be used to define security settings that apply to:
opts: All Pods within a specific Namespace.
A specific container within a Pod.
All containers within a Pod, and potentially the Pod's volumes.
Only the network policies associated with a Pod.
The ServiceAccount used by the Pod.
expl: A PodSecurityContext defines privilege and access control settings for an entire Pod. These settings apply to all containers within that Pod and can also affect volumes (e.g., fsGroup). A SecurityContext can also be set at the individual container level.

q: What is a key benefit of using the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) from Kubernetes' perspective?
opts: It simplifies the container image building process for developers.
It allows Kubernetes to be independent of specific container runtime implementations.
It provides a built-in metrics collection system for containers.
It enables running virtual machines alongside containers within the same Pod.
It standardizes the format for container log messages.
expl: CRI provides a stable abstraction layer. This allows Kubernetes to support multiple container runtimes without having runtimespecific code in the core kubelet. As long as a runtime implements CRI, Kubernetes can use it.

q: What is the main difference between iptables and IPVS modes for kube-proxy?
opts: iptables mode is newer and supports more advanced features.
IPVS is designed for smaller clusters; iptables for larger ones.
iptables uses linked lists for rules; IPVS uses hash tables, often better for scale.
IPVS mode does not require kube-proxy to run on worker nodes.
iptables mode can only handle HTTP traffic; IPVS handles all TCP/UDP.
expl: IPVS (IP Virtual Server) is built on the Netfilter hook function and uses hash tables. It's generally considered more performant and scalable for a large number of Services compared to iptables mode, which uses sequential rule processing that can become slow with many rules.

q: When would you typically choose a Service Mesh over just using Kubernetes NetworkPolicies for securing inter-Pod communication?
opts: When you need basic L3/L4 firewalling based on Pod labels.
When you require application-layer (L7) traffic management, mTLS, and observability.
When you need to expose services externally using an Ingress controller.
When you want to restrict a Pod's access to host resources.
When the primary concern is node-to-node encryption.
expl: NetworkPolicies provide L3/L4 segmentation between Pods. A Service Mesh operates at L7, offering richer features such as automatic mTLS for strong workload identity and encryption, fine-grained traffic control (retries, timeouts, traffic splitting, circuit breaking), and detailed telemetry (metrics, logs, distributed traces) for application traffic. You choose a mesh when those L7 capabilities are required.

q: How does a VolumeSnapshot object in Kubernetes relate to a PersistentVolumeClaim?
opts: It defines the maximum size a PVC can grow to.
It's a request to create a point-in-time copy of the data in a PVC.
It's a type of PVC that uses ephemeral local storage.
It specifies the encryption key to be used for a PVC.
It provides a template for creating multiple identical PVCs.
expl: A VolumeSnapshot represents a snapshot of the data on a volume associated with a PVC at a specific point in time. This is typically used for backup and restore purposes and requires a CSI driver that supports snapshots.

q: Which Kubernetes security mechanism would you use to prevent containers in a Pod from running as the root user or gaining new privileges?
opts: NetworkPolicy
Role and RoleBinding
SecurityContext (e.g., runAsNonRoot: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false)
Secret storing user credentials.
LimitRange defining CPU limits.
expl: The SecurityContext (at Pod or container level) allows setting various security parameters. runAsNonRoot: true ensures the container doesn't run as root. allowPrivilegeEscalation: false prevents a process from gaining more privileges than its parent.

q: What is the primary advantage of a "headless" Service in Kubernetes?
opts: It provides a stable ClusterIP for load balancing.
It automatically creates an external load balancer for the Service.
It allows direct Pod IP discovery via DNS, often used with StatefulSets.
It encrypts all traffic to the Pods it selects.
It exposes Pods on every node using a fixed NodePort.
expl: A headless Service (clusterIP: None) does not get a ClusterIP. Instead, DNS queries for the Service name return the IP addresses of the individual backing Pods. This is useful for peer-to-peer discovery or when clients need to connect to specific Pod instances, common with StatefulSets.

q: In a Service Mesh, what is the typical role of the "data plane"?
opts: To define global policies and configurations for service communication.
To collect telemetry data and send it to a centralized monitoring system.
To actually handle and proxy network traffic between services (e.g., using sidecars).
To provide a user interface for managing and visualizing the mesh.
To manage the lifecycle of service instances (deploy, scale, heal).
