expl: Requests are used for scheduling and reserve a guaranteed share of node resources, while limits cap the maximum a container can use. When limits greatly exceed requests, containers can burst beyond their guaranteed share and starve other Pods on the node — leading to CPU throttling, OOM kills, and Pod eviction once the kubelet detects node pressure.

q: When Prometheus fails to scrape a target (e.g., due to network issues or the target being down), what value will the up metric for that target typically have?
opts: NaN (Not a Number)
The metric will be absent (no data point recorded).
expl: The up metric explicitly indicates scrape success or failure. A value of 1 means the scrape succeeded, while 0 means it failed (timeout, connection refused, non-200 HTTP response, etc.). The metric is not absent and not NaN — it's specifically set to 0.

q: Which of the following is NOT a typical characteristic of cloud native applications?
opts: Self-healing through automated restarts and rescheduling
Loosely coupled services that can be deployed independently
Tightly coupled, monolithic deployment units sharing a single runtime
Designed for observability with metrics, logs, and traces
expl: Cloud native applications are resilient (self-healing), loosely coupled (so services can scale and evolve independently), and observable. Tight coupling and monolithic deployment is the opposite of the cloud native model — it prevents independent scaling, slows release cadence, and creates large blast radii on failure.

q: Select advantages of cloud native architecture
opts: Tightly coupled monoliths and manual deployments
High automation and elastic scalability
Mandatory use of proprietary hardware
Single-vendor lock-in by design
expl: Cloud native architectures emphasize high automation (declarative orchestration, CI/CD, GitOps) and elastic scalability (horizontal scaling, autoscaling). The other choices describe characteristics cloud native explicitly seeks to avoid.

q: The Open Container Initiative standards are for
opts: Runtime, image and build
Runtime, image and distribution
Image, build and distribution
Storage, build and image
expl: OCI publishes three specifications: Runtime Spec (how to execute a container), Image Spec (how images are packaged), and Distribution Spec (how images are pushed/pulled via registries).

q: Cloud applications are usually scaled by
opts: Vertical scaling
Horizontal scaling
Diagonal scaling
Manual provisioning
expl: Horizontal scaling — adding more instances behind a load balancer — is the dominant model for stateless cloud native apps. Vertical scaling (bigger machines) is bounded by hardware limits and typically causes downtime.

q: Serverless computing does not require
opts: Servers
Provisioning and operating infrastructure
Application Code
Network
expl: Servers still exist under the hood — the word 'serverless' means developers don't provision or operate them. The platform handles capacity, patching, scaling, and runtime lifecycle.

q: The twelve-factor app is a guideline to
opts: Develop cloud native applications
Build container images
Deploy applications on Kubernetes
Design hardware networking topologies
expl: The 12-factor methodology (originated at Heroku) is a set of guidelines for building portable, scalable, cloud-suited applications — config in env vars, logs as streams, disposability, and so on. It predates Kubernetes and is independent of any container build tooling.

q: Which of the following is not a standard used for container orchestration?
opts: Container Network Interface (CNI)
Container Deployment Interface (CDI)
Container Runtime Interface (CRI)
Container Storage Interface (CSI)
expl: CNI (networking), CRI (runtime), and CSI (storage) are real Kubernetes interface standards. 'Container Deployment Interface (CDI)' is a distractor — deployment is handled by controllers, not a standardized plugin interface.

q: Which of the following is not a key metric for Site Reliability Engineers?
opts: Service level agreement (SLA)
Service level indicator (SLI)
Service level objective (SLO)
Service level requirement (SLR)
expl: SRE uses SLIs (what you measure), SLOs (internal targets), and SLAs (external contractual promises). 'SLR' is not an SRE term.

q: Containers can help with which of the following? (best answer)
opts: Packaging an application with its dependencies for consistent execution across environments
Automatically generating secure application source code
Eliminating the need for an operating system kernel
Replacing the need for a CI/CD pipeline
expl: Containers package an application together with its dependencies and runtime configuration into a portable artifact, solving the 'works on my machine' problem and enabling consistent execution across dev, test, and prod. They share the host kernel and do not write secure code or replace CI/CD.

q: The usage of containers and virtual machines are mutually exclusive. True or False?
opts: True
False
expl: False. Containers commonly run inside VMs — many managed Kubernetes services provision VM worker nodes and run containers on top. The two technologies complement rather than replace each other.

q: Container isolation is achieved by which Linux kernel features?
opts: Namespaces and cgroups
Container Network Interface
Security Groups and Firewall
Service Mesh
expl: Linux namespaces isolate what a process can see (PID, network, mount, user, UTS, IPC). Cgroups limit what a process can use (CPU, memory, I/O). Together they're the core primitives behind container isolation.

q: A Dockerfile contains instructions on how to do what?
opts: Install Docker
Start multiple containers
Install Kubernetes
Build a container image
expl: A Dockerfile is the declarative build recipe for a container image — base image, copied files, installed packages, entrypoint. `docker build` reads it and produces an OCI image.

q: Which command can be used to build a container image with Docker?
opts: docker build
docker run
docker commit
docker pull
expl: `docker build` reads a Dockerfile and produces a container image. `docker run` starts a container from an existing image, `docker commit` creates an image from a running container's filesystem changes, and `docker pull` downloads an existing image from a registry.

q: Sort the 4C's of cloud native security, starting from the inside.
opts: Code, container, cloud, cluster
Cluster, container, cloud, code
Code, container, cluster, cloud
Container, cluster, cloud, code
expl: The 4C's of Cloud Native Security, from innermost layer outward: Code → Container → Cluster → Cloud. Each layer secures the next one out; weaknesses in an inner layer can't be fully compensated by outer layers.

q: Which problems can be solved with a container orchestration system?
opts: Scheduling containers across nodes
Managing CPU and memory resources
Providing a cluster network for containers
Writing application source code
expl: Container orchestrators like Kubernetes schedule workloads onto nodes, enforce resource requests/limits, and provide a cluster network (via a CNI plugin). They do not write or generate application source code — that remains a developer responsibility.

q: What do you call the automatic process of discovering services on a network?
opts: Service Mesh
Service Registry
Service Discovery
Service IP
expl: Service discovery is the mechanism by which clients locate service instances dynamically — in Kubernetes, via DNS lookups on Service names. A service mesh *uses* service discovery but adds a proxy layer on top.

q: What are the main parts of a Service Mesh?
opts: Ingress and Egress
Data Plane and Control Plane
Proxy and Registry
Sidecar and Gateway
expl: A service mesh is composed of a data plane (sidecar proxies that intercept and route actual service-to-service traffic) and a control plane (which configures the proxies with policies, certificates, and routing rules).

q: Kubernetes was originally designed and developed by
opts: Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF)
Apache Software Foundation
Linux Foundation Networking
Open Container Initiative (OCI)
expl: Kubernetes originated at Google (based on lessons from Borg) and was donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) in 2015. CNCF, a sub-foundation of the Linux Foundation, hosts and stewards the project today.

q: Which of the following is NOT primarily a tool for setting up a Kubernetes cluster?
opts: Minikube
kubeadm
Helm
kops
expl: Minikube (local single-node), kubeadm (production-grade bootstrap), and kops (cluster lifecycle on cloud) all create Kubernetes clusters. Helm is a package manager for applications running on Kubernetes — it does not provision the cluster itself.

q: Sort the three stages a request needs to go through in the api-server.
opts: Admission Control, Authorization, Authentication
Authentication, Admission Control, Authorization
Authentication, Authorization, Admission Control
Authorization, Authentication, Admission Control
expl: The API server processes requests in this order: Authentication (who are you?) → Authorization (are you allowed?) → Admission Control (should this specific object be accepted or mutated?).

q: Which container runtime is marked as deprecated by Kubernetes?
opts: CRI-O
containerd
Docker
gvisor
expl: Direct Docker support via dockershim was deprecated in Kubernetes 1.20 and removed in 1.24. Kubernetes now talks to OCI-compliant runtimes via the CRI — containerd (which Docker itself uses under the hood) and CRI-O are standard choices.

q: Which Kubernetes component is responsible for scheduling?
opts: kube-apiserver
kube-scheduler
kube-controller-manager
kube-proxy
expl: The kube-scheduler watches for pods without an assigned node and picks one based on resource requests, affinity, taints, and other constraints.

q: Kubernetes manifests are most commonly written in which data-serialization format?
opts: TOML
XML
YAML
INI
expl: Kubernetes manifests are canonically written in YAML. JSON is also accepted by the API, but YAML is preferred for its readability and support for comments. TOML, XML, and INI are not used by the Kubernetes API.

q: Which of the following is not a required field in a Kubernetes object?
opts: kind
spec
containers
metadata
expl: The required top-level fields in a Kubernetes manifest are apiVersion, kind, metadata, and spec (for most resources). `containers` is a field nested inside a Pod's spec, not a top-level required field.

q: Which kubectl subcommand applies a manifest declaratively, creating or updating resources to match the file?
opts: kubectl create
kubectl apply
kubectl run
kubectl expose
expl: kubectl apply is the declarative command — it creates resources that don't exist and updates ones that do, tracking changes via the last-applied-configuration annotation. kubectl create is imperative and fails if the resource exists; run starts a pod; expose creates a Service from an existing resource.

q: Which pod construct runs to completion before the main application containers start, and blocks the pod from starting if it fails?
opts: Sidecar container
Init container
Ephemeral container
postStart lifecycle hook
expl: Init containers run sequentially to completion before app containers start; if any init container fails, the pod is restarted (or fails) per its restartPolicy. Sidecars run alongside the main container, ephemeral containers are injected for debugging a running pod, and postStart hooks run after a container has already started.

q: Which workload object does not exist in Kubernetes?
opts: ReplicaSet
Deployment
ApplicationSet
StatefulSet
expl: ReplicaSet, Deployment, StatefulSet (and DaemonSet, Job, CronJob) are built-in Kubernetes workload resources. 'ApplicationSet' is an Argo CD CRD, not a core Kubernetes workload.

q: Which of the following is NOT a Kubernetes Service type?
opts: ClusterIP
NodePort
Ingress
ExternalName
expl: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and ExternalName are the four Service types. Ingress is a separate API resource (managed by an Ingress controller) that routes HTTP/S traffic to Services — it is not itself a Service type.

q: Ingress objects can be used to configure HTTP(S) routing rules. True or False?
opts: True
False
expl: True. Ingress is the Kubernetes resource for L7 HTTP/S routing — host- and path-based rules, TLS termination. An Ingress controller (NGINX, Traefik, etc.) reads these rules and implements them.

q: Which Kubernetes object can be used to request storage?
opts: Container Storage Interface
PersistentVolume
PersistentVolumeClaim
StorageClass
expl: A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is how a user/Pod requests storage. It binds to a matching PersistentVolume (PV), which represents the actual storage. StorageClass defines how PVs are dynamically provisioned; CSI is the plugin interface.

q: Which statement best describes the difference between ConfigMaps and Secrets?
opts: Secrets are intended for sensitive data and are base64-encoded, but are not encrypted at rest by default
Secrets are encrypted at rest by default while ConfigMaps are stored in plain text
ConfigMaps are namespaced while Secrets are cluster-scoped
Secrets can only hold strings while ConfigMaps can hold any data type
expl: Secrets are designed for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys) and their values are base64-encoded for binary safety — but base64 is encoding, not encryption. Encryption at rest in etcd is opt-in and must be configured separately. Both ConfigMaps and Secrets are namespaced and can hold arbitrary key-value data.

q: Which Kubernetes autoscaler adjusts a workload's CPU and memory requests/limits rather than the number of replicas?
opts: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)
Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)
Cluster Autoscaler
Karpenter
expl: VPA tunes pod resource requests/limits based on observed usage. HPA scales the number of replicas, while Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter add or remove nodes — they operate on different axes from VPA.

q: In a GitOps workflow, what does a commit to the main branch of the configuration repository typically represent?
opts: A trigger to rebuild the container runtime
The desired state of the system, which a controller will reconcile toward
A direct API call to the Kubernetes control plane
An immutable container image
expl: GitOps treats the Git repository as the single source of truth for desired state. A commit changes that desired state, and an in-cluster agent (e.g., Argo CD, Flux) continuously reconciles the live cluster to match. Commits do not call the API directly or build images by themselves.

q: Which best describes the GitOps approach to managing systems?
opts: Using Git purely as a backup mechanism for cluster snapshots
Manually applying kubectl commands and recording them in Git afterward
Declaring desired state in Git and using an automated agent to reconcile the cluster
Pushing container images directly from developer laptops to production
expl: GitOps stores the declarative desired state (manifests, Helm values, infrastructure code) in Git, and an automated controller continuously pulls and applies changes to keep the live system in sync. Manual kubectl drift, ad-hoc backups, and laptop-to-prod pushes are anti-patterns it is meant to replace.

q: Which of the following best describes a CI/CD pipeline?
opts: A storage location for source code and configuration
A defined sequence of automated stages (e.g., build, test, deploy) that an artifact passes through
A network connection between developer machines and the cluster
A long-lived branch dedicated to production releases
expl: A CI/CD pipeline is the automated sequence of stages — typically build, test, scan, and deploy — that code moves through on its way to production. Repositories store code, branches organize history, and networks carry traffic, but none of these define the automation flow itself.

q: Flux and ArgoCD are popular GitOps tools. They use a push-based approach.
opts: True
False
expl: False. Flux and ArgoCD are pull-based: an agent runs inside the cluster and pulls desired state from Git, reconciling continuously. Pull is considered more secure because the cluster doesn't need inbound credentials from CI.

q: Observability is the same as monitoring. True or False?
opts: True
False
expl: False. Monitoring checks predefined signals against thresholds ('known unknowns'). Observability lets you ask arbitrary questions about system state from its outputs (logs, metrics, traces) — addressing 'unknown unknowns'.

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three pillars of observability?
opts: Metrics
Logs
Alerts
Traces
expl: The three pillars of observability are metrics, logs, and traces. Alerts are a downstream consumer of these signals — they are produced by evaluating metrics or logs, but are not themselves a pillar.

q: How can you show the logs of a previously terminated container named ruby in the web-1 pod?
opts: kubectl logs web-1 ruby
kubectl logs -p web-1 ruby
kubectl web-1 ruby -p
kubectl logs -p -c ruby web-1
expl: Use `-p` (--previous) to fetch logs from the prior instance of a container that has since restarted or terminated. Use `-c` to specify a container when the pod has multiple. Correct: `kubectl logs -p -c ruby web-1`.

q: What do you call the pattern where you add a second container to your pod to collect and ship logs?
opts: Cluster-level logging
Node-level logging
Sidecar container logging
Application-level logging
expl: The sidecar pattern adds an auxiliary container to the pod. For logging, a sidecar can tail the app's log files and ship them to an aggregator — without modifying the main application.

q: Which format is most widely adopted for structured application logs?
opts: Plain unstructured text
JSON (one object per line)
Syslog RFC 3164
CSV
expl: Line-delimited JSON is the de facto structured logging format — compact, machine-parseable, and supported by all major aggregators (Loki, Elasticsearch, CloudWatch, Datadog). Plain text lacks structure, syslog has limited fields, and CSV is awkward for nested or optional data.

q: What kind of software is Prometheus?
opts: Software to manage containers
Software to collect and store metrics
Software to manage virtual machines
Software to collect and store logs
expl: Prometheus is a time-series database and monitoring system for metrics. It scrapes HTTP endpoints, stores the data in its TSDB, and exposes PromQL for querying and alerting.

q: Which Prometheus metric type should be used for a value that only increases (e.g., an error_count)?
opts: Histogram
Gauge
Counter
Summary
expl: Counters monotonically increase (or reset to zero on restart). Use them for event counts like requests, errors, or bytes sent. Gauges go up and down; histograms/summaries track distributions.

q: What is the front end for the Kubernetes control plane?
opts: kube-scheduler
etcd
kube-controller-manager
kube-apiserver
expl: The kube-apiserver is the only component that talks to etcd and is the entry point for all cluster interactions. Every other component (schedulers, controllers, kubelets, kubectl) communicates through it.

q: Which key-value store is used as Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data?
opts: etcd
kube-scheduler
kube-apiserver
kubelet
expl: etcd is the distributed, consistent key-value store that holds all cluster state. Lose etcd = lose the cluster. Production clusters run etcd as a 3- or 5-node cluster for high availability.

q: Which component watches newly created pods that have no node assigned, and selects a node for them to run on?
opts: kube-proxy
kube-scheduler
kube-apiserver
kubelet
expl: The kube-scheduler is specifically responsible for this. It filters candidate nodes (that can satisfy the pod's requests) and scores them, then binds the pod to the best node via the API server.

q: Which of the following runs controllers?
opts: kube-proxy
kube-scheduler
kube-apiserver
kube-controller-manager
expl: The kube-controller-manager bundles core controllers — Node, ReplicaSet, Deployment, Endpoints, ServiceAccount, and more — into a single binary. Each controller runs a reconciliation loop.

q: Which agent runs on each node in a Kubernetes cluster?
opts: etcd
kube-scheduler
kube-apiserver
kubelet
expl: kubelet runs on every node. It talks to the API server, receives pod specs assigned to its node, and instructs the container runtime (via CRI) to actually run the containers.

q: Which set of Kubernetes components makes global decisions about the cluster, such as scheduling and responding to events?
opts: Control plane components
Node components
Add-ons
Container runtime
expl: The control plane (API server, scheduler, controller manager, etcd) makes cluster-wide decisions. Node components (kubelet, kube-proxy) execute work locally on each node. Add-ons provide optional cluster features like DNS or dashboards, and the container runtime runs individual containers.

q: Which of the following is NOT a typical Kubernetes deployment target?
opts: A managed cloud service such as EKS, GKE, or AKS
An on-premises datacenter using kubeadm or a distribution
A local developer workstation using Minikube or kind
A web browser running JavaScript
expl: Kubernetes runs on Linux nodes, so it can be deployed in managed cloud, on-prem, and local-dev environments. A web browser cannot host the Kubernetes control plane or workloads — Kubernetes is not a browser runtime.

q: Which of the following tools is NOT typically used to run a local single-node or single-host Kubernetes cluster for development?
opts: Minikube
kind
k3d
kube-proxy
expl: Minikube, kind (Kubernetes-in-Docker), and k3d (k3s-in-Docker) are all popular tools for running a local Kubernetes cluster on a developer machine. kube-proxy is a per-node networking component inside any Kubernetes cluster — it does not provision clusters.

q: Which statement about Kubernetes autoscaling is most accurate?
opts: Cluster Autoscaler is provider-agnostic and works identically on every cloud without configuration
HPA and VPA work on any conformant Kubernetes cluster, while Cluster Autoscaler relies on cloud-provider integrations to add or remove nodes
Autoscaling is only supported on Google Cloud
HPA scales nodes, while Cluster Autoscaler scales pod replicas
expl: HPA (replicas) and VPA (resource requests/limits) are provider-agnostic and run on any Kubernetes cluster with a metrics source. Cluster Autoscaler scales the node pool itself and therefore needs a provider-specific integration (AWS, GCP, Azure, on-prem auto-provisioners, etc.).

q: Which best describes a container image?
opts: A running process executing on a node
A Kubernetes Pod specification stored in etcd
An immutable artifact that bundles an application with its runtime dependencies
A kernel module loaded on the host
expl: A container image is an immutable, layered artifact (typically OCI-formatted) that packages an application binary together with its libraries, files, and configuration so it runs consistently across environments. A running process is a container, a Pod spec is API metadata, and kernel modules live in the host kernel.

q: Which of the following is the most accurate statement about local Kubernetes development tools?
opts: Minikube is the only tool that can run Kubernetes on a developer workstation
kind runs Kubernetes nodes as Docker containers, making it well-suited for CI environments
Docker Desktop cannot run a Kubernetes cluster
k3d requires a separate hypervisor and cannot run on Linux
expl: kind (Kubernetes IN Docker) runs each Kubernetes node as a Docker container, which makes it fast to start and tear down — ideal for CI pipelines. Minikube, Docker Desktop, and k3d are all valid local options as well, each with different tradeoffs around VM usage, multi-node support, and resource overhead.
